elements.sent-2411-489: An organization allows its employees to remotely connect to servers within the organization's internal network to perform their daily tasks and access remote services.
elements.sent-2411-490: The organization operates in two regions: one in Poland and the other in the US.
elements.sent-2411-491: Each remote developer has access to three IP address ranges: 10.0.0.1/32 for their personal server, 10.0.0.2/32 for a shared server (e.g., an internal mail server), and 128.252.0.0/16, which is accessed via the organization’s servers.
elements.sent-2411-492: Each user is assigned a dedicated IP-based address space, meaning that two users can send traffic to the same IP address (e.g., 10.0.0.1), but the physical destinations of the traffic are different.
elements.sent-2411-493: Thus, the user-destination association is unique.
elements.sent-2411-494: The organization requires a solution that enforces end-to-end policies and provides seamless updates to endpoints and address spaces.
elements.sent-2411-495: In this specific case, a user residing in Poland has moved to the US but is still connecting to an ingress address and a personal server located in Poland.
elements.sent-2411-496: The overlay controller and the user detected an increase in latency.
elements.sent-2411-497: The policy enforces that end-to-end latency should not exceed a certain threshold, and if this threshold is violated, the control plane can adjust overlay parameters to improve the end-user experience.
elements.sent-2411-498: In this scenario, the controller decides to relocate both the ingress point and the user’s personal server (10.0.0.1) to the US to improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-499: The organization can set up this use case by using WireGuard as the VPN client and server.
elements.sent-2411-500: As part of the server configuration, traffic from each user can be routed to a specific IP table to resolve the destination based on the user’s assigned IP, providing dedicated IP-based address space.
elements.sent-2411-501: Additional configurations, such as source NAT, may also be required for a fully functional setup.
elements.sent-2411-502: However, this approach presents several challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-503: WireGuard traffic is easily detectable and can be blocked by any ISP along the path.
elements.sent-2411-504: At the organization’s ingress, there needs to be a VPN server to terminate the VPN tunnels and forward traffic to the final destination.
elements.sent-2411-505: As a result, the VPN tunnel is not truly end-to-end.
elements.sent-2411-506: Since the VPN tunnel is not end-to-end, certain policies cannot be enforced.
elements.sent-2411-507: For instance, a remotely connected CEO may require end-to-end encrypted access to personal servers, while this may not be necessary for a junior developer.
elements.sent-2411-508: The current implementation cannot satisfy this requirement.
elements.sent-2411-509: Updating this configuration is slow and burdensome.
elements.sent-2411-510: If the company changes the VPN server address, every user's VPN profile needs to be updated.
elements.sent-2411-511: Similarly, changing a user’s destination server requires modifying numerous IP table rules within the VPN server.
elements.sent-2411-512: For example, if the company needs to route user traffic to different endpoints during working hours and after hours (e.g., changing the physical location of server 10.0.0.1 after working hours), technical challenges and the lack of a smooth update process may hinder the implementation of such features.
elements.sent-2411-513: With traditional VPN protocols, authentication, authorization, policy enforcement, and cryptographic aspects are limited to the VPN's specification and implementation.
elements.sent-2411-514: However, the company needs more control over the choices it can make.
elements.sent-2411-515: For example, it might decide that for some users there is no need for IP-level packet encryption since applications can use TLS.
elements.sent-2411-516: For other users, it might choose TLS encryption between overlay nodes to encrypt traffic.
elements.sent-2411-517: For another group of users, it might decide to use obfuscation to prevent ISP blocking.
elements.sent-2411-518: All of these options will be severely limited both in implementation and performance if the company chooses traditional VPN protocols.
elements.sent-2411-519: This use case involves utilizing Hermes as a connectivity facilitator, where the organization acts as the communication provider, enabling remote users to connect with the internal network.
elements.sent-2411-520: The overlay will not be configured for a specific service but instead provide efficient IP-level connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-521: As previously discussed, proxies listen to UDP and TCP endpoints, and tunnel devices are needed to redirect general IP packets, such as ICMP, to proxy endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-522: To enable this scenario, two features are required:.
elements.sent-2411-523: Encapsulation of IP traffic in TCP or UDP.
elements.sent-2411-524: Assigning each destination network (within the user’s dedicated address space) to a specific port of the user’s local proxy and sending encapsulated traffic to it (split tunneling).
elements.sent-2411-525: Envoy proxy cannot perform these actions and needs to be assisted by other components.
elements.sent-2411-526: Therefore, we developed an assisting component to provide both IP-in-UDP encapsulation and split tunneling: we create tunnels at end-user devices and destinations, encapsulate IP traffic in UDP packets, and deliver the encapsulated packets to the end-user local proxy UDP endpoint based on the destination network address.
elements.sent-2411-527: The local proxy encapsulate UDP in HTTP, appends user-related HTTP headers to associate destination addresses with the user's identity, and forwards the traffic to the ingress, which acts as an HTTP proxy.
elements.sent-2411-528: The ingress performs HTTP-level parsing and determines the next destination based on the HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-529: As a result, there is no tunnel termination at the ingress, and the tunnel remains active until the traffic reaches its final destination, where the tunnel is terminated.
elements.sent-2411-530: The overlay can selectively enable TLS links between each proxy if it is configured, which enables encrypted end-to-end communication.
elements.sent-2411-531: Additionally, the overlay can perform policy-based routing and authorization using the HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-532: The network configuration is shown in Figure \ref{fig:overall .}
elements.sent-2411-534: In this example, the ingress proxy acts as a dependent proxy since it directly receives traffic from destination addresses.
elements.sent-2411-535: There are two users, each running a tunneling client that functions as an assisting component to their local proxy.
elements.sent-2411-536: User2 is located in the US, while User1, along with all ingress points and servers, is in Poland.
elements.sent-2411-537: The proxy configurations are shown in Table \ref{IPconfig .}
elements.sent-2411-538: This experiment was performed over the internet and not in a controlled network.
elements.sent-2411-539: Please note that we did not configure TLS-encrypted links between overlay nodes since the experiment uses Ping to measure latency, and encryption is unnecessary.
elements.sent-2411-540: The proxy assisting components were configured to send traffic destined for 10.0.0.1/32, 10.0.0.2/32, and 128.252.0.0/16 to three distinct UDP ports on the users' local proxies.
elements.sent-2411-541: The local proxy listens on these ports and encapsulates incoming UDP packets in HTTP using MASQUE.
elements.sent-2411-542: It also appends two HTTP headers: "user" and "IPaddress," which contain information about the user and the destination address.
elements.sent-2411-543: For example, a realization of these headers could be "user":"user2" and "IPaddress":"128.252.0.0." The "IPaddress" represents the destination network address, and the overlay uses the "user" and "IPaddress" headers to route traffic to the appropriate node.
elements.sent-2411-544: The local proxy then sends the HTTP-encapsulated UDP packets to the ingress proxy.
elements.sent-2411-545: At the ingress, the HTTP headers are parsed to determine the next node, and the HTTP encapsulation is removed, leaving the UDP packet.
elements.sent-2411-546: The UDP packet is then forwarded to the appropriate tunnel endpoint based on the user and destination information in the HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-547: At the tunnel endpoint, the UDP packet is decapsulated to extract the original IP packet.
elements.sent-2411-548: The original IP packet is then routed to its final destination.
elements.sent-2411-549: The update process consists of three steps.
elements.sent-2411-550: The initial step, shown in Figure \ref{fig:IP1 , depicts User2, who is located in the US, connecting to an ingress point and a personal server, both located in Poland.}
elements.sent-2411-551: We initially have one ingress point and all users are connected to it.
elements.sent-2411-552: Since we want to move both the ingress point and the personal server (10.0.0.1) to the US, we first dynamically update the end-user proxy configuration to point to a new ingress point in the US.
elements.sent-2411-553: This allows us to migrate the personal server’s content to the US.
elements.sent-2411-554: Therefore, in the first step shown in Figure \ref{fig:IP2 , the controller initiates the update by reconfiguring User2’s proxy to send traffic to the new ingress point in the US.}
elements.sent-2411-555: Once the new personal server is ready, in the second step shown in Figure \ref{fig:IP3 , the controller updates the new ingress server to route traffic destined for the personal server (10.0.0.1) to the newly migrated server in the US.}
elements.sent-2411-556: We evaluate performance by measuring the Round-Trip Time (RTT) of ICMP ECHO packets (Ping).
elements.sent-2411-557: The initial latencies are shown in Tables \ref{tab:IPinitial .}
elements.sent-2411-558: Table \ref{tab:IPinitial shows the RTT values when we ran Ping to send packets every second.}
elements.sent-2411-559: The latency column measures how far the servers were from User2, who, as previously mentioned, resides in the US.
elements.sent-2411-560: The "Latency with tunnel" columns describe the latency when only IP-in-UDP encapsulation is used, and traffic is sent directly to the destination tunnel's UDP endpoint.
elements.sent-2411-561: As shown, IP-in-UDP encapsulation introduces a subtle performance penalty, but as seen in the last column, encapsulating UDP in HTTP and routing traffic over the overlay adds more to the latency.
elements.sent-2411-562: This is because, before sending the very first ICMP packet, proxies need to establish a TCP connection, which adds to the latency.
elements.sent-2411-563: However, as shown in Table \ref{tab:IPinitial , overlay routing adds up to 5 ms to the latency, which is still negligible.}
elements.sent-2411-564: To better measure the effectiveness of the updates, we sent ICMP packets every 10 seconds and waited up to 3 seconds for a response.
elements.sent-2411-565: Due to the timeout configuration of 3 seconds, as listed in Table \ref{IPconfig , each ping required a new TCP connection, resulting in higher latency compared to the base measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-566: We also updated User 2's Envoy proxy version to 1.32.0 \cite{envoy_132 and set the max\_stream\_duration to 3 seconds to prevent Envoy crashes.}
elements.sent-2411-567: The update events are shown in Figures \ref{fig:IPresult along with the corresponding RTT values.}
elements.sent-2411-568: Initially, the average latency was 382.33 Ms. After the first update, latency dropped to 206.33 ms as the ingress point was moved closer to the user.
elements.sent-2411-569: Following the second update, the average RTT further decreased to 79.68 ms, as both the ingress point and the personal server were now closer to the user.
elements.sent-2411-570: Please note that the results in Table \ref{tab:IPinitial were measured by sending PING packets every second, while the results in Figure \ref{fig:IPresult were measured by sending PING packets every 10 seconds.}}
elements.sent-2411-571: There were no dropped packets, and the entire update process for each proxy was completed in less than 10 seconds from initiating the update, including a five-second sleep cycle for the controller to check for configuration changes.
elements.sent-2411-572: As shown, the overlay proxy can route IP packets with the help of assisting components, demonstrating that the Hermes architecture can handle general IP routing and serve a variety of use cases.
elements.sent-2411-573: It's important to note that using the architecture only requires split tunneling and the encapsulation of IP packets in either UDP or TCP.
elements.sent-2411-574: Other solutions, such as proxying IP over HTTP (MASQUE \cite{schinazi_masque_2024 and RFC 9484), can be used to send IP packets directly to an HTTP endpoint, which would significantly reduce the overhead associated with the UDP tunneling approach used here.}
elements.sent-2411-575: However, the current implementation of MASQUE is still in its early stages \cite{connectIPFailed , preventing us from using it for this scenario.}
elements.sent-2411-576: Additionally, we still require split tunneling to associate each destination network with a specific port on the local proxy.
elements.sent-2411-577: A WireGuard client and server can also be used as assisting components if they are configured to send traffic to local proxies.
elements.sent-2411-578: However, as mentioned above, this approach adds unnecessary encryption and increases the complexity of the solution.
elements.sent-2411-579: If there is a need for encrypted end-to-end communication, the solution presented here can be modified to use TLS-encrypted links between proxies.
elements.sent-2411-580: Although we observed a 5 ms increase in latency between the user and the US server when connected through the proxy, we also noted the architecture's notable performance in facilitating communication.
elements.sent-2411-581: The architecture was able to easily update each user's address space, a task that might be very complex with other implementations.
elements.sent-2411-582: In addition, we gained complete control over IP packet routing by utilizing HTTP headers added at the end-user proxies, rather than relying solely on IP-level data.
elements.sent-2411-583: This allowed us to enforce policies at the IP level and perform IP routing based on user identity, not just IP destinations.
elements.sent-2411-584: Indeed, Hermes enabled dedicated address spaces where the IP address 10.0.0.1 could route to different physical machines for different users.
elements.sent-2411-585: Finally, both updates took less than 10 seconds from the time the configuration database was updated until the proxies finished hot restarting, including a five-second sleep cycle for the controller to check configuration changes.
elements.sent-2411-586: This allowed us to quickly respond to network condition changes and adapt new end-to-end routes for User2 in under 10 seconds.
elements.sent-2411-587: It should be noted that there was no change in IP table rules and the only change was in the overlay configuration.
elements.sent-2411-588: So far, we have demonstrated how Hermes can improve service delivery and act as a communication facilitator.
elements.sent-2411-589: Now, we explore a use case where a multi-domain Hermes overlay is used as the communication pipeline between NDN components, showing that it can provide NDN namespace load balancing without the need to configure NDN nodes directly.

elements.sent-2411-590: An organization has decided to adopt the NDN architecture \cite{zhang_named_2014 for some of its branches worldwide.}
elements.sent-2411-591: NDN nodes use Faces \cite{shi_what_2021 to connect to other nodes over the internet.}
elements.sent-2411-592: Faces are responsible for encapsulating and decapsulating NDN data over TCP or UDP, which makes the data routable over the internet.
elements.sent-2411-593: The organization has opted to use UDP Faces, which requires specifying the addresses of nodes in other branches to enable communication.
elements.sent-2411-594: The organization aims to provide two namespaces, "DEV" and "TEST," with load balancing between the servers hosting each namespace.
elements.sent-2411-595: Since this is an experimental architecture, the organization does not want to expose these UDP ports to the internet.
elements.sent-2411-596: Additionally, the organization has a limited pool of dynamic IP addresses and no static IP addresses, complicating network configuration and maintenance.
elements.sent-2411-597: In this use case, we assume the existence of a multi-domain overlay of standalone proxies, which are already functional.
elements.sent-2411-598: These proxies have been configured to process "service" and "namespace" HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-599: For this use case, the "service" equals "NDN," and the "namespace" can be either "DEV" or "TEST.".
elements.sent-2411-600: Hermes can directly map each NDN namespace to a dedicated address space.
elements.sent-2411-601: To implement this, NDN Forwarding Daemons (NFD) should send requests for each namespace to a specific port on the local dependent proxy.
elements.sent-2411-602: The organization must then configure end-users' dependent proxies to map the NDN namespaces to the overlay address spaces by appending the appropriate HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-603: Autonomous domains will process these HTTP headers to route the traffic to its final destination.
elements.sent-2411-604: The overlay proxies can use DNS to locate the next destination, with only HTTP ports being exposed, reducing the risk of exposing UDP ports.
elements.sent-2411-605: The organization simply needs to configure its dependent proxies and add agreed-upon headers between domains.
elements.sent-2411-606: This enables the organization to leverage the benefits of the multi-domain overlay for load balancing and routing.
elements.sent-2411-607: The network configuration is shown in Figure \ref{fig:ndn .}
elements.sent-2411-608: The end-user used ndncatchunks \cite{ndncatput to request content from the DEV and TEST namespaces, while content was published on servers using ndnputchunks \cite{ndncatput .}}
elements.sent-2411-609: As depicted in Figure \ref{fig:ndn , apart from the end-user and servers, no other nodes used NFDs.}
elements.sent-2411-610: The end-user’s NFD was configured to send interests for each namespace to distinct ports of its local dependent proxy, which was running on a separate machine.
elements.sent-2411-611: This decision—to run the end-user’s dependent proxy on another machine—was made to help regulate NDN’s outgoing traffic.
elements.sent-2411-612: Once the local proxy received the UDP packets, it tunneled them over HTTP and appended two headers: “service: NDN” and a namespace header, either “namespace: DEV” or “namespace: TEST,” depending on the port through which it received the data.
elements.sent-2411-613: In this way, an NDN namespaces is mapped to an overlay address space.
elements.sent-2411-614: The data was then forwarded to the Domain 1 ingress proxy, which processed only the “service: NDN” header.
elements.sent-2411-615: It subsequently forwarded the traffic to the Domain 2 proxy, which parses the “namespace” header.
elements.sent-2411-616: Domain 2's proxy then distributed the traffic between the internet-facing organization's HTTP proxies.
elements.sent-2411-617: When the traffic reached the organization's HTTP proxies, they terminated the HTTP tunnel and delivered the UDP traffic to the local NFDs.
elements.sent-2411-618: Since all NFDs only communicated with their respective local proxies, their configurations remained simple, and there was no need to configure them to balance the load between available servers.
elements.sent-2411-619: In the direct NDN connection (Figure \ref{fig:ndn -bottom), the client knew the addresses of the servers, and its NFD was configured with those addresses.}
elements.sent-2411-620: We published three files with approximate sizes of 1MB, 26MB, and 49MB, and measured the Transfer Time, which is the total time it takes for ndncatchunks to receive the requested file.
elements.sent-2411-621: Additionally, we published a 0KB file to measure latency.
elements.sent-2411-622: We repeated the experiments at least 20 times, requesting both namespaces twice in each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-623: The average results for both namespaces are reported in Table \ref{tab:ndn .}
elements.sent-2411-624: Although the Hermes approach is up to 12\% slower than the direct approach, and its latency is slightly higher, it provides global reachability and load balancing, facilitating the development and usage of the NDN architecture while maintaining reliability, even when NDN uses UDP faces.
elements.sent-2411-625: As demonstrated, Hermes can serve as the underlying communication network for other experimental protocols and architectures.
elements.sent-2411-626: These systems need only to deliver their data units to their local proxy, after which Hermes manages reliability, load balancing, namespace routing, and other concerns.
elements.sent-2411-627: In the multi-domain overlay, no node has knowledge of the full network topology, and data is delivered through hop-by-hop HTTP processing.
elements.sent-2411-628: This approach greatly simplifies NDN network configuration and management.
elements.sent-2411-629: Moreover, more complex traffic patterns, such as multicast, can also be supported by the overlay, as proxies function as IP endpoints with complete control over traffic.
elements.sent-2411-630: Additionally, proxies can leverage assisting components to generate more advanced traffic patterns, further enhancing network capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-631: Please note that the overlay creates a one-to-one mapping between NDN namespaces and Hermes address spaces, treating each namespace as a separate address space.
elements.sent-2411-632: This allows NDN developers to focus on the development of their applications with confidence, knowing that Hermes can efficiently handle the NDN networking aspects.

elements.sent-2411-633: So far, we have discussed how Hermes can facilitate service delivery and communication over the internet by providing an overlay of reconfigurable proxies, delegating network responsibilities to them, utilizing HTTP tunneling along with HTTP semantic-based processing, and incorporating assisting components.
elements.sent-2411-634: In this section, we will compare Hermes with other related architectures and solutions.
elements.sent-2411-635: Hermes is comparable to architectures that use an overlay of proxies.
elements.sent-2411-636: Although Hermes is designed as a general-purpose architecture, we have chosen to compare it with service mesh architectures, which are specialized for managing services.
elements.sent-2411-637: This comparison is particularly relevant because both rely on an overlay of proxies, and Envoy, the proxy we used to prototype Hermes, is also commonly used in service mesh implementations like Istio \cite{istio .}
elements.sent-2411-638: The comparison is primarily based on Istio and Linkerd \cite{linkerd implementations and is summarized in Table \ref{tab:servicemesh .}}
elements.sent-2411-639: As shown, the Hermes and service mesh architectures are inherently different.
elements.sent-2411-640: A service mesh, as its name suggests, provides an overlay specifically for service components within a controlled environment.
elements.sent-2411-641: In contrast, the Hermes creates an overlay that extends from end-user devices and may terminate at service components, the public internet, or other networking architectures.
elements.sent-2411-642: Because of these differences in scope, their goals and consequently the features they provide are different.
elements.sent-2411-643: For example, a Hermes overlay cannot be deployed using a service mesh, as end-users (the traffic sources) are external to the service environment.
elements.sent-2411-644: In a service mesh, the infrastructure is typically fully controlled and runs in a managed environment.
elements.sent-2411-645: However, the Hermes operates over the internet, where there is often no control over the entire end-to-end path due to the nature of how internet routing works.
elements.sent-2411-646: More importantly, a service mesh is usually designed to handle and process TCP and HTTP traffic, whereas the Hermes is built to carry general IP packets and encapsulated traffic from various other architectures.
elements.sent-2411-647: These fundamental differences result in distinct concepts, features, and use cases for each architecture.
elements.sent-2411-648: Please note that, since Hermes is a general-purpose architecture, one of its applications can be extending a service mesh to end-user devices, thereby enabling end-to-end service provisioning and improving resource allocation by incorporating real-time end-user statistics.
elements.sent-2411-649: Although there has been some research on using service meshes in edge systems \cite{elkhatib_evaluation_2023 and as a networking layer \cite{ashok_leveraging_2021 in cloud-native environments, these applications still align with the comparison made in Table \ref{tab:servicemesh , as they were not used for general-purpose networking.}}}
elements.sent-2411-650: Instead, they focused on specific use cases within managed or controlled environments, further highlighting the distinct goals and capabilities of Hermes compared to service mesh architectures.
elements.sent-2411-651: A more relevant comparison would be between Hermes and performance-enhancing proxies (PEP) as well as other general-purpose overlay approaches.
elements.sent-2411-652: A PEP can improve the performance of internet protocols at the transport and application layers.
elements.sent-2411-653: The effectiveness of PEPs in reducing end-to-end delay and packet loss in satellite communication and cellular environments has been demonstrated in studies such as \cite{ehsan_evaluation_2003, caini_pepsal_2006, abdelsalam_quic_proxy_2019, ivanovich_tcp_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-654: Additionally, the authors of \cite{ciko_pep-dna_2021 showed how PEPs can be used to connect the internet to other clean-slate architectures, such as Content-Centric Networking (CCN) \cite{jacobson_networking_2009 and Recursive internetwork Architecture (RINA) \cite{trouva_transport_2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-655: Their approach involves breaking the end-to-end TCP logic and translating TCP packets into new architecture formats using a proxy.
elements.sent-2411-656: Designing a translator for every new architecture may not be a practical solution.
elements.sent-2411-657: Instead, encapsulation and decapsulation can be used as alternatives to translation: data units are encapsulated within a transport protocol that the internet supports, delivered across the internet, and decapsulated at the destination.
elements.sent-2411-658: This approach allows new architectures to be designed as overlay networks that leverage the internet's best-effort delivery service.
elements.sent-2411-659: Some notable examples of such overlay networks include the Tor network as an anonymous communication network, Akamai as a performance-enhancing overlay network, and I-3 \cite{stoica_internet_2002 as a general-purpose networking architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-660: Among these, both Tor and I-3 use their own specific data formats but rely on the internet to carry data between overlay nodes.
elements.sent-2411-661: In recent research, the authors of \cite{mccauley_enabling_2019 introduced Trotsky, an architectural framework aimed at making the internet more extensible.}
elements.sent-2411-662: Trotsky decouples interdomain routing from intradomain routing and establishing an overlay layer 3.5 between the internet domains.
elements.sent-2411-663: We can summarize the comparison of the Hermes with PEPs and general-purpose overlay approaches in Table \ref{tab:related .}
elements.sent-2411-664: Both PEP-DNA and COIN \cite{jahanian_managing_2020 rely on translating between architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-665: Supporting multiple architectures with this method requires numerous translation gateways, which is burdensome and prone to errors or incomplete translations.
elements.sent-2411-666: A more efficient approach is to encapsulate custom data formats within the payload of UDP, TCP, or HTTP packets and deliver them to the local proxy, as we did with NDN faces in Section \ref{NDN .}
elements.sent-2411-667: The overlay will then manage reliable end-to-end data delivery and routing.
elements.sent-2411-668: Akamai uses multiple layers of DNS resolvers to redirect traffic to one of its edge servers, requiring it to publish DNS resolution information and update it globally.
elements.sent-2411-669: In contrast, the Hermes relies on proxies to handle name and address resolution based on their configuration.
elements.sent-2411-670: Proxies can either resolve a name locally or forward the request to another proxy.
elements.sent-2411-671: This approach eliminates the need for global DNS updates, as name resolution can be performed at specific overlay proxies, streamlining the process and reducing the need for global-scale DNS management.
elements.sent-2411-672: I-3 decouples senders and receivers by associating packets with IDs, making it one of the first attempts to separate networking knowledge from applications.
elements.sent-2411-673: Similarly, in Hermes, senders and receivers are not directly coupled and communicate only with their nearest dependent proxies.
elements.sent-2411-674: However, the Hermes approach is more general, allowing for complete decoupling of networking knowledge and even enabling protocol translation without the sender or receiver's awareness.
elements.sent-2411-675: While I-3 introduces a new packet format, Hermes uses the TCP/IP protocol suite, and its functionality can be extended by implementing HTTP-based semantics.
elements.sent-2411-676: Trotsky is not a complete architecture; it is an architectural framework that primarily enforces logical pipes between layer 3.5 components and provides a host bootstrapping mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-677: Essential features of a layer 3.5 implementation, such as naming, addressing, security, and interlayer mapping, are left to be defined by specific layer 3.5 designs.
elements.sent-2411-678: In contrast, the Hermes is a fully defined architecture that specifies the complete treatment of packets from start to finish using the TCP/IP protocol suite, with mechanisms and behavior determined by the overlay configuration.
elements.sent-2411-679: As shown, Hermes is fundamentally different from other related research.
elements.sent-2411-680: The concepts used in its design make Hermes the first overlay of proxies that can be applied to general-purpose use cases.
elements.sent-2411-681: This paper proposed Hermes, a general-purpose networking architecture that creates an overlay of reconfigurable dependent and standalone proxies, managed through a control plane.
elements.sent-2411-682: We explored its core concepts and features, showing how Hermes enhances end-to-end control over service delivery and communication by delegating networking responsibilities to the overlay, utilizing TCP, UDP, and HTTP proxying, relying on HTTP processing for routing and implementing new functionalities, and incorporating assisting components.
elements.sent-2411-683: Through prototyping, we demonstrated Hermes’ applicability across various use cases.
elements.sent-2411-684: We showed that Hermes can provide backward compatibility, support reliable delivery in unstable networking conditions, enable end-to-end policy-aware IP traffic control, and act as a communication pipeline between components of future networking architectures and protocols.
elements.sent-2411-685: In future work, we plan to explore the capabilities of the Hermes overlay in addressing various policy-based traffic management and routing use cases.
elements.sent-2411-686: Additionally, we will examine how efficient end-to-end resource allocation can be achieved using Hermes architecture.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}
elements.sent-2411-687: All prototype implementations and use cases we presented in this paper are available at address https://github.com/Bfarkiani/Hermes.

elements.sent-2411-689: Louis, MO, 63130, USA Emails: \{b.farkiani, fan.liu, k.yang1, jdd, jp, pcrowley\}@wustl.edu \markboth {IEEE Transactions on Network and Service Management {Farkiani \MakeLowercase{et al. : Hermes: A general-purpose proxy-enabled networking architecture.}}}
elements.sent-2411-690: We introduce Hermes, a general-purpose networking architecture that aims to improve service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-691: Hermes delegates networking responsibilities from applications and services to proxies and is designed as a portable, adaptable solution to four fundamental challenges of efficient service delivery over the Internet: end-to-end traffic management, backward compatibility, data-plane security and privacy, and adaptable communication layers.
elements.sent-2411-692: The design centers on an overlay of reconfigurable proxies and HTTP tunneling and proxying techniques, utilizing assisting components to extend proxy functionality when needed.
elements.sent-2411-693: Through prototyping and emulation, we demonstrate that Hermes improves key performance metrics across multiple use cases: it provides backward compatibility through protocol translation and tunneling, improves reliability by delegating retry logic to proxies, enables unified policy-based Layer 3 routing across network segments, and serves as an efficient substrate for future architectures like NDN, facilitating their operation over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-694: Beyond evaluating Hermes across various use cases, we measured the overhead of Hermes' HTTP tunneling and proxying mechanisms and found it to be modest, typically under 2 ms per hop.
elements.sent-2411-695: With workloads of up to 1000 concurrent requests, we also show that Hermes proxies can amortize connection setup time and reduce end-to-end latency compared to direct no-proxy baselines.
elements.sent-2411-696: By offering only best-effort packet delivery, the Internet can interconnect independently administered networks at low cost.
elements.sent-2411-697: However, efficient service delivery over the Internet faces several challenges.
elements.sent-2411-698: BGP is a policy-based routing protocol that prioritizes peering agreements over performance metrics, often resulting in suboptimal routing paths.
elements.sent-2411-699: Additionally, the Internet employs location-based addressing rather than content-centric models.
elements.sent-2411-700: These challenges, along with minimal built-in security considerations, have hindered efficient service delivery over the Internet and highlighted the need to revisit and improve the Internet's foundational design and service architectures \cite{balakrishnan_revitalizing_2021, brown_architecture_2024, clark_overlay_2006, mccauley_enabling_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-701: Over the years, numerous solutions have been proposed to improve service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-702: Some advocate for complete clean-slate replacements of the global network architecture, including SCION \cite{perrig_scion_2017 and Named Data Networking (NDN) \cite{zhang_named_2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-703: Despite their contributions and innovations, these clean-slate Internet replacements have not achieved widespread adoption \cite{mccauley_enabling_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-704: Furthermore, designing applications for architectures fundamentally different from the Internet requires rethinking communication models, APIs, and deployment practices, changes that may not be justifiable given the paradigmatic differences and the enormous installed base of existing Internet infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-705: Other approaches aim to improve specific aspects of Internet service delivery without replacing the underlying architecture.
elements.sent-2411-706: These innovations include performance-enhancing proxies (PEPs) \cite{griner_performance_2001 , application-layer solutions such as Content Distribution Networks (CDNs) and Fog/Edge computing, and both public and private overlay networks like Tor \cite{dingledine_tor_2004 and Akamai \cite{nygren_akamai_2010 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-707: PEPs represent one of the earliest efforts to improve reliability over lossy paths.
elements.sent-2411-708: They operate transparently at the transport layer by splitting end-to-end connections, buffering packets, providing enhanced congestion control, and supporting forward error correction.
elements.sent-2411-709: While PEPs transparently serve 20 to 40\% of Internet paths \cite{yuan_sidekick-network_2024 , they also function as bridges between incompatible architectures; for instance, PEP-DNA \cite{ciko_pep-dna_2021 translates between IP and NDN models.}}
elements.sent-2411-710: In contrast to transport-layer PEPs, application-layer approaches such as CDNs, edge computing, and fog computing place data and computation closer to end-users and improve performance metrics.
elements.sent-2411-711: Many CDN and edge systems use overlay routing, directing traffic across dedicated networks optimized for service-specific delivery rather than relying on default Internet routing.
elements.sent-2411-712: For example, both Akamai \cite{nygren_akamai_2010 and Cloudflare \cite{cloudflare_interconnect, argo_smartrouting operate their own overlay networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-713: Overlay networks vary in their traffic ingress and egress points and in the specific benefits they provide.
elements.sent-2411-714: Tor \cite{dingledine_tor_2004 is a public overlay designed primarily for anonymity.}
elements.sent-2411-715: Users run Tor clients on their devices, and traffic is routed through volunteer-operated relays to an exit relay for public sites, or to the service itself in the case of onion services.
elements.sent-2411-716: Conversely, private overlays like Akamai typically route traffic from an intermediate node near the source to one near the destination \cite{nygren_akamai_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-717: PEPs, CDNs, edge/fog computing, and many other advances, together with end-to-end protocol innovations such as QUIC \cite{iyengar_quic_2021 and HTTP/3 \cite{bishop_http3_2022 , have materially improved service delivery over the Internet.}}
elements.sent-2411-718: Without these advances, today's Internet would likely not function effectively at scale.
elements.sent-2411-720: In this paper, we identify four structural limitations that affect efficient service delivery over the Internet and motivate our work.
elements.sent-2411-721: The first is end-to-end traffic management.
elements.sent-2411-722: Most existing solutions focus on the service-provider domain, targeting improvements within infrastructure under provider control while neglecting the end-user side.
elements.sent-2411-723: These solutions tend to bypass the last mile, the network segment that reaches end-user devices.
elements.sent-2411-724: This segment experiences most performance degradation \cite{fontugne_persistent_2020, bajpai_dissecting_2017, brown_architecture_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-725: Instead, existing solutions focus on upstream segments where performance can be more easily optimized due to less heterogeneity and greater control.
elements.sent-2411-726: The second limitation is backward compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-727: While services evolve rapidly, customer clients may not be able to evolve at the same pace.
elements.sent-2411-728: This mismatch leads to fragmentation in the customer base and slow adoption of new services.
elements.sent-2411-729: The third limitation concerns security and privacy models.
elements.sent-2411-730: Various and often conflicting models exist across the path that handles end-user traffic, both across network segments and across network layers: IP reachability rules, middlebox filtering, and application-layer authorization are each defined and enforced in isolation, with no shared notion of identity or policy continuity.
elements.sent-2411-731: The fourth limitation is the lack of an adaptable communication layer.
elements.sent-2411-732: Systems that are built on top of fixed protocols and assumptions inherit their limitations, and these limitations propagate into any solution layered above them.
elements.sent-2411-733: When those limitations conflict with service goals, service providers cannot easily change the communication layer itself and are instead forced to add workarounds in the application.
elements.sent-2411-734: A communication layer that can adapt to network conditions and service requirements without modifying application logic would directly address this gap.
elements.sent-2411-735: Our goal is to provide a unified end-to-end solution to these challenges to improve service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-736: We design our solution as a general-purpose architecture so service providers can apply it across different use cases.
elements.sent-2411-737: As a general-purpose solution, it must handle any type of traffic at the IP layer and above, and it must be extensible to support new functionality.
elements.sent-2411-738: It must also be adaptable, meaning that after deployment, data-plane configuration can be updated to address changes in network conditions and service requirements over time.
elements.sent-2411-739: Finally, it must be portable, meaning it can be used to address various use cases with minimal architectural change.
elements.sent-2411-740: Service providers can use our solution to gain end-to-end control of routing, policy, and observability from user devices to services.
elements.sent-2411-741: This improves service performance and simplifies cross-layer policy management without requiring changes to application logic.
elements.sent-2411-742: Our solution can be deployed selectively for a subset of users or services that need special traffic handling, and it coexists with and complements other mechanisms that providers already use, such as CDNs and service meshes \cite{farkiani_service_2022 .}

elements.sent-2411-743: We build a general-purpose architecture named Hermes using the following elements.
elements.sent-2411-744: First, we delegate networking tasks from application logic to centralized components, namely proxies.
elements.sent-2411-745: Proxies can be updated independently to react to network and service changes without disrupting applications.
elements.sent-2411-746: Next, we deploy an overlay of proxies from end-user devices to wherever the service requires.
elements.sent-2411-747: For example, an end-to-end overlay can extend from end-user devices to a service mesh that handles service-to-service communication.
elements.sent-2411-748: This overlay controls traffic at the source and enables fine-grained routing, resource allocation, and security and privacy management across the Internet and network segments.
elements.sent-2411-749: Because Hermes needs to be general-purpose, it must handle IP traffic.
elements.sent-2411-750: We therefore extend standard proxy functionality with assisting components such as tunnel devices to capture and process IP traffic.
elements.sent-2411-751: We support multiple proxy deployment options, enabling Hermes to operate in a wide range of settings.
elements.sent-2411-752: An architecture must specify its data-plane protocols.
elements.sent-2411-753: Hermes aims to be portable, so it relies only on UDP, TCP, and HTTP proxying and tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-754: These protocols are ubiquitous, and HTTP tunneling allows encapsulation of other protocols using the MASQUE framework \cite{schinazi_masque_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-755: We select HTTP as the backbone of the architecture.
elements.sent-2411-756: Using HTTP makes Hermes easily extensible; new functionality can be introduced by defining custom HTTP headers, provided the logic exists in proxies and assisting components.
elements.sent-2411-757: A logically centralized control plane, operated by the overlay operator (either the service owner or a third party), manages the Hermes overlay.
elements.sent-2411-758: A vision of the architecture is shown in Figure \ref{fig:vision .}
elements.sent-2411-759: Here, the service provider operates its own Hermes overlay over the Internet, and the overlay is integrated with the service provider's service mesh.
elements.sent-2411-760: This creates an end-to-end overlay that carries traffic from the user device to the target service.
elements.sent-2411-761: The service provider uses different cloud providers to host Hermes proxies across the Internet in various regions.
elements.sent-2411-762: When user traffic requires third-party services, Hermes proxies can forward requests to external CDNs and receive traffic back from them.
elements.sent-2411-763: This paper makes two main contributions.
elements.sent-2411-764: First, it introduces the design and components of Hermes.
elements.sent-2411-765: We developed a prototype using commercially relevant open-source projects and custom software, including an overlay controller and Android and iOS applications.\footnote{The code for this paper is available at: \url{https://github.com/Bfarkiani/Hermes Although we describe control-plane components and their features, our focus is on the data plane: what the components are, which features they require, and how they operate to address challenges.}}
elements.sent-2411-766: Second, by evaluating the prototype developed in this work, we show that Hermes:.
elements.sent-2411-767: Additionally, because Hermes relies on HTTP tunneling and HTTP-layer semantic processing, we evaluate multiple tunneling and proxying approaches and find that the one-hop processing overhead is at most 2ms.
elements.sent-2411-768: We also evaluate using workloads of up to 1000 concurrent connections and show that proxies can even reduce end-to-end latency compared with direct no-proxy approaches by utilizing HTTP/2 multiplexing and connection pooling.
elements.sent-2411-769: Section \ref{related explores the challenges and related work in greater detail, and Section \ref{hermes describes Hermes components, features, and how Hermes addresses challenges.}}
elements.sent-2411-770: In Section \ref{perf , we investigate the performance of HTTP proxying and tunneling approaches that Hermes uses.}
elements.sent-2411-772: We first explore the challenges Hermes addresses in detail, and then discuss related architectures and solutions.. End-to-end traffic management.
elements.sent-2411-773: In practice, providers rely on DNS and BGP to route end-user traffic to their infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-774: DNS caching and long TTLs, along with BGP's policy-based path selection, limit fast per-flow steering and inhibit fine-grained per-customer control.
elements.sent-2411-775: Therefore, providers usually have little leverage over traffic before it reaches their ingress, and they defer control decisions until traffic reaches ingress.
elements.sent-2411-776: A prominent example is rate limiting.
elements.sent-2411-777: HTTP APIs, the dominant channel for Internet service delivery, are usually protected by provider-side rate limiters that define and enforce quotas.
elements.sent-2411-778: Because providers have little or no control over when end users send requests, clients typically resort to exponential backoff when they receive HTTP 429 responses or, in some services, 503 responses (for example, Amazon \cite{amazon_ratelimit and OpenAI \cite{openai_exponential ) after exceeding service quotas.}}
elements.sent-2411-779: When an endpoint is shared by uncoordinated clients, independent exponential backoff wastes retry attempts because users lack visibility into aggregate load, as we showed in \cite{farkiani_rethinking_2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-780: Generally, having end-to-end control over traffic can improve routing, resource management, and service delivery.
elements.sent-2411-781: For example, PAINTER \cite{koch_painter_2023 showed that end-to-end traffic management can help in enterprise networks: an enterprise-managed edge proxy tunnels outbound flows to selected cloud ingress points based on live measurements and policy, reducing latency and improving resilience.}
elements.sent-2411-782: However, PAINTER targeted enterprise cloud deployments with controllable edges such as SD-WAN stacks, rather than the general-purpose endpoints addressed in this paper.. Backward compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-783: Services continuously evolve, including updates to both application logic and client-facing interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-784: Interface evolution, in particular, requires updating how users access service endpoints, often across a wide range of dedicated clients and even web browsers.
elements.sent-2411-785: Given the diversity of user devices and software stacks, developers must ensure consistent behavior across all environments.
elements.sent-2411-786: However, this is challenging for two reasons: (1) the environments in which end-users run their clients are often heterogeneous, making it difficult to achieve consistent behavior across all configurations; and (2) users may simply refuse to update their hardware or software.
elements.sent-2411-787: As a result, service providers often face fragmented customer bases, where some users can access newer services while others remain dependent on legacy paths, requiring continued support for both.
elements.sent-2411-788: A notable example is Server Name Indication (SNI) for TLS-encrypted communication.
elements.sent-2411-789: During secure connection setup, the server must return the correct certificate.
elements.sent-2411-790: Without SNI, virtual hosting requires mapping each hostname to a separate IP address.
elements.sent-2411-791: SNI enables multiple destinations to share the same IP address and allows the server to select the appropriate certificate based on the unencrypted SNI field, thereby reducing cost and improving scalability.
elements.sent-2411-792: Although SNI was introduced in 2003 as part of RFC 3546 \cite{blake-wilson_transport_2003 , deployment lagged for years because many clients, especially Internet Explorer 6 on Windows XP, lacked SNI support.}
elements.sent-2411-793: As XP's share declined in the mid-2010s, SNI became safe to rely on broadly \cite{sni_story, sni_story2 .}
elements.sent-2411-795: Modern deployments implement a patchwork of security and privacy mechanisms that operate under fundamentally different models across network layers and network segments.
elements.sent-2411-796: For example, service mesh implementations like Istio \cite{istio_l4 offer Layer 4-7 zero-trust policy enforcement independent of the underlying infrastructure \cite{farkiani_service_2022, li_service_2019 , whereas Layer 3 policies depend entirely on platform support.}}
elements.sent-2411-797: Layer 3 policies control what addresses can communicate with each other, irrespective of any application logic at upper layers.
elements.sent-2411-798: Layer 3 policy enforcement is a standard feature in many infrastructures.
elements.sent-2411-799: For example, in Kubernetes, which currently has an 83\% market share \cite{redhat_kub , it can be enforced using network plugins \cite{kubernetes_plugin .}}
elements.sent-2411-800: Kubernetes by default allows entities to communicate freely, even across tenant boundaries, making Layer 3 policy enforcement necessary.
elements.sent-2411-801: However, 91\% of clusters do not implement any network policies \cite{wiz_report, fuad_intent-based_2024 , leaving Layer 3 unprotected.}
elements.sent-2411-802: Therefore, authorization must be fully implemented for data-plane endpoints according to application logic, which is a complicated process considering the size of service components.
elements.sent-2411-803: This lack of unified policy enforcement across network layers makes the application layer vulnerable, since application developers might incorrectly presume a safe Layer 3 and leave application layer authorization incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-804: Furthermore, treatment of traffic varies significantly before and after the ingress point.
elements.sent-2411-805: User traffic may be unencrypted or protected only by simple TLS (i.e., server certificate plus application-layer credentials) whereas service-to-service traffic typically uses mutual TLS.
elements.sent-2411-806: Privacy guarantees also vary, as data may be decrypted, re-encrypted, and stored multiple times along the path before it reaches the target service, increasing the risk of exposure.. Adaptable communication layer.
elements.sent-2411-807: Services often rely on fixed communication protocols and assumptions that have inherent design limits.
elements.sent-2411-808: For example, the Internet is primarily location-addressed, so any service built on top of it is forced to run over location-based addressing.
elements.sent-2411-809: If a service prefers content-based addressing, it must implement content naming and lookup in the application layer.
elements.sent-2411-810: If these features were already present in the communication layer, service complexity would be greatly reduced.
elements.sent-2411-812: Transports such as TCP and QUIC provide congestion control and loss recovery, but services that rely only on these standard designs cannot easily and dynamically customize core behaviors beyond what the specifications expose.
elements.sent-2411-813: As another example, if providers rely only on QUIC and HTTP/3, for example, to improve client mobility and avoid HTTP/2 head-of-line blocking, users may experience up to 45\% worse performance than HTTP/2 over TCP on high-speed links \cite{zhang_quic_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-814: All protocol designs have inherent limitations, so a communication layer with predetermined protocol choices cannot perform well in all conditions, especially on the Internet where conditions fluctuate rapidly and unpredictably.
elements.sent-2411-815: Services therefore benefit from a communication layer that adapts to service needs and network conditions and can update its protocols and configurations to avoid limitations that hurt service delivery.
elements.sent-2411-816: Such a layer should be extensible based on service needs, reconfigurable and policy-driven, and able to steer traffic according to application and network requirements.
elements.sent-2411-817: To the best of our knowledge, there is no general-purpose solution that addresses these challenges in a unified way.
elements.sent-2411-818: In our prior work \cite{farkiani_rethinking_2025 , we addressed HTTP API rate limiting by introducing proxies that manage client retries.}
elements.sent-2411-819: These proxies can be implemented as service workers \cite{service_workers in web browsers, and we showed that using proxies reduced HTTP errors by up to 97\%.}
elements.sent-2411-820: In addition, in \cite{farkiani_l3mesh we demonstrated that assisting components can enable portable service mesh implementations such as Istio \cite{istio to handle Layer 3 network policies in a portable, infrastructure-agnostic way.}}
elements.sent-2411-821: More importantly, by building a lightweight Layer 3 overlay that added less than 1ms to end-to-end latency, we showed that service meshes can handle Layer 3 through Layer 7 traffic management and policy enforcement in an integrated way over diverse infrastructures.
elements.sent-2411-822: This paper generalizes our earlier work to address the fundamental challenges of efficient service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-823: Hermes targets service providers as its primary users.
elements.sent-2411-824: It is not intended as a replacement for the Internet or for legacy protocols, unlike architectures such as SCION, NDN, or Trotsky \cite{mccauley_enabling_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-825: Instead, Hermes leverages widely deployed Internet protocols to remain portable while providing a diverse range of benefits.
elements.sent-2411-826: From the perspective of end-to-end traffic management, Hermes can be compared to Tailscale \cite{tailscale , Cloudflare Zero Trust \cite{cloudflare_zerotrust , and OpenZITI \cite{openziti .}}}
elements.sent-2411-827: These systems also extend from end-user devices using proxies and tunneling techniques and provide a general-purpose secure access layer.
elements.sent-2411-828: Hermes provides a broader feature set than these architectures.
elements.sent-2411-829: In addition to providing a secure end-to-end access layer, Hermes integrates with service logic to act as a reliable and adaptable end-to-end communication layer that directly addresses service requirements.

elements.sent-2411-830: From a design viewpoint, as Hermes is composed of an overlay of proxies, it is comparable to architectures that use proxy overlays.
elements.sent-2411-831: The most closely related are service mesh architectures, which specialize in managing service-to-service communication.
elements.sent-2411-832: Although various service mesh implementations exist with different features, including Istio \cite{istio , Linkerd \cite{linkerd , and Cilium mesh \cite{cilium_mesh , the features listed in Table~\ref{tab:servicemesh are common among them.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-833: As shown, Hermes and service mesh architectures are inherently different.
elements.sent-2411-834: Although some research has explored using service meshes in edge systems \cite{elkhatib_evaluation_2023 and as a networking layer \cite{ashok_leveraging_2021 in cloud-native environments, service meshes are not designed for general-purpose networking and operate within managed and controlled environments.}}
elements.sent-2411-835: In contrast, Hermes creates an overlay that extends from end-user devices and may terminate at service components, the public Internet, or other networking architectures.
elements.sent-2411-836: Hermes does not assume the service provider has control over the end-user devices where proxies run, except for proxy configuration.
elements.sent-2411-837: For this reason, we designed Hermes to be portable with various deployment options so it can operate in diverse settings.
elements.sent-2411-838: Additionally, Hermes operates over the Internet, where there is often no control over the entire end-to-end path due to the nature of Internet routing.
elements.sent-2411-839: More importantly, service meshes are typically designed to handle TCP and HTTP traffic, whereas Hermes is built to carry general IP packets and encapsulates traffic from various architectures using HTTP tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-840: Notably, one application of Hermes is extending service meshes to end-user devices, enabling end-to-end service provisioning and improving resource allocation by incorporating real-time end-user statistics.
elements.sent-2411-841: Hermes can also be compared with other overlay architectures like Akamai.
elements.sent-2411-842: CDN platforms like Akamai are not end-to-end; they steer end-user traffic using DNS-based resolution techniques to one of their edge points.
elements.sent-2411-843: In contrast, with Hermes, end-users do not need to resolve a name to an IP address, and their traffic is captured directly at the end-user device.
elements.sent-2411-844: As we show in Section~\ref{hermes , clients can submit a name to a proxy, and proxies forward it until it reaches a proxy that is configured to resolve it to a destination address.}
elements.sent-2411-845: This reduces the burden of globally consistent name resolution while preserving the provider's control over policy and routing.
elements.sent-2411-846: Another relevant design comparison is between Hermes and performance-enhancing proxies (PEPs).
elements.sent-2411-847: PEPs can improve the performance of Internet protocols at the transport and application layers.
elements.sent-2411-848: Additionally, the authors of \cite{ciko_pep-dna_2021 showed how PEPs can be used to connect the Internet to other clean-slate architectures, such as Content-Centric Networking (CCN) \cite{jacobson_networking_2009 and Recursive Internetwork Architecture (RINA) \cite{trouva_transport_2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-849: Hermes proxies indeed act as PEPs.
elements.sent-2411-850: Additionally, they use encapsulation and decapsulation of IP packets in HTTP via the MASQUE framework to carry any type of traffic without requiring protocol translation.
