elements.sent-2411-851: We summarize the comparison of Hermes with PEPs and related approaches in Table~\ref{tab:related .}
elements.sent-2411-852: Hermes uses an alternative approach: encapsulating custom data formats within the payload of UDP, TCP, or HTTP packets and delivering them to proxies.
elements.sent-2411-853: Overlay proxies then manage reliable end-to-end data delivery and routing.
elements.sent-2411-854: Compared to Akamai, Hermes is an end-to-end architecture that manages end-user traffic directly from their devices.
elements.sent-2411-855: Tailscale and other zero trust approaches also provide only a subset of the features that Hermes provides, as discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-856: Note that Hermes does not replace current CDN and edge service providers such as Akamai, Cloudflare, and Fastly, or architectures that unify access to edge services like InterEdge \cite{brown_architecture_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-857: Service providers use these offerings as building blocks for the services they deliver to end-users.
elements.sent-2411-858: Hermes complements them by providing end-to-end control for the service provider across the public Internet.
elements.sent-2411-859: For example, a provider may use CDNs to reduce latency while using Hermes to control how end-user traffic reaches the CDN edge endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-860: If reliability issues arise, Hermes can enforce remedies at the source.
elements.sent-2411-861: Finally, Hermes specifies the capabilities proxies should provide and how they operate to address concrete challenges.
elements.sent-2411-862: Each service provider instantiates its own proxy configurations to meet specific needs, which in turn define how traffic is processed.
elements.sent-2411-863: Hermes targets service-delivery problems under the assumption that providers do not control end-user devices and that traffic traverses the public Internet with limited control.
elements.sent-2411-864: Once traffic enters the provider's controlled infrastructure, Hermes is neutral: the provider decides whether proxies are still needed, how to implement proxies, and where traffic should be delivered.
elements.sent-2411-865: Hermes integrates with existing infrastructure and is agnostic to deployment models inside controlled environments (e.g., containers, VNFs, physical appliances) and to lower-layer routing choices (e.g., SD-WAN, VNF-FG, VPNs).
elements.sent-2411-866: This neutrality makes Hermes a complementary solution that focuses on challenges current tools cannot address while fitting alongside existing provider infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-867: Additionally, Hermes proxies deployed within managed infrastructure can be treated as standard service components and scaled horizontally as needed.
elements.sent-2411-868: We first introduce the core concepts and solution elements we use in the design of Hermes.
elements.sent-2411-869: Then we discuss how Hermes addresses the challenges.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{elements.pdf} \caption{The solution elements used in the design of Hermes and their relations.} \label{fig:elements}
elements.figure-2411-026: \caption{Mapping goals to solution elements} \label{tab:goal_map} \begin{tabular}{|l|l|} \multicolumn{1}{|c|}{Goal} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Solution element} Portability \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Relying on UDP, TCP, and HTTP proxying and tunneling Relying on user-space solutions Various proxy deployment options\end{tabular} Adaptability \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Reconfigurable proxies and assisting components that handle Layer 3-7 traffic\end{tabular} General-purpose \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Handling IP traffic using tunnel devices Relying on HTTP headers for new semantics\end{tabular} End-to-end \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}An overlay of proxies extended from end-user devices\end{tabular} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-870: Hermes is a general-purpose networking architecture that is composed of an overlay network of dynamically reconfigurable proxies and managed by an overlay control plane.
elements.sent-2411-871: The elements we used in the design of Hermes and their relations are shown in Figure \ref{fig:elements .}
elements.sent-2411-872: Table \ref{tab:goal_map shows how each element is related to the Hermes goals.}
elements.sent-2411-873: Hermes builds on the idea that the separation of networking logic from application logic, which has been valuable in service mesh solutions \cite{farkiani_service_2022, li_service_2019 , can also benefit general-purpose networking.}
elements.sent-2411-874: This enables applications to communicate with fixed endpoints (i.e., proxies) without worrying about networking aspects, as the endpoint handles all networking complexities.
elements.sent-2411-875: Additionally, this separation enables fast adaptation to network and service changes without modifying application logic.
elements.sent-2411-876: However, a notable difference between service meshes and Hermes is that traffic redirection in service meshes is usually done transparently through iptables \cite{istio_redirect or kernel-based \cite{cilium_redirect approaches.}}
elements.sent-2411-877: Hermes targets end-user devices, where we assume the service provider does not have control over the software and hardware except for proxy configuration through the control plane.
elements.sent-2411-878: Therefore, traffic must be explicitly redirected to proxies either by sending traffic directly to the proxy endpoint, configuring applications to use proxies, or using tunnel devices that capture related traffic.
elements.sent-2411-879: This highlights the second element, which is proxies and assisting components.
elements.sent-2411-880: For example, Tor \cite{dingledine_tor_2004 uses proxies to create an anonymous overlay network that preserves privacy, while Cloudflare \cite{cloudflare_work employs reverse proxies to enhance Internet security, performance, and reliability.}}
elements.sent-2411-881: Proxies designed for the Internet architecture process traffic at the transport or application layer and serve as forwarding agents \cite{fielding_hypertext_1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-882: In Hermes, we extend proxy capabilities with assisting components like caching mechanisms, advanced traffic manipulation algorithms, policy enforcement components, and most importantly, tunnel devices.
elements.sent-2411-883: Tunnel devices capture traffic at Layer 3 and deliver it to proxies for service-specific processing.
elements.sent-2411-884: In addition, Hermes requires proxies to be reconfigurable, i.e., their configuration, including the protocols and ports they use, can be updated at any time through the control plane.
elements.sent-2411-885: This enables the overlay of proxies to become adaptable.
elements.sent-2411-886: We define two roles for proxies: Dependent proxies (DP) and Standalone proxies (SaP).
elements.sent-2411-887: Dependent proxies receive traffic directly from the source or destination of the traffic.
elements.sent-2411-888: SaPs act as routers and connect DPs together.
elements.sent-2411-889: We examine their details in Section \ref{components .}
elements.sent-2411-890: As Hermes aims to enable end-to-end solutions, proxies are mandatory components at the end-user side of the communication.
elements.sent-2411-891: The overlay proxies thus start from end-user devices and can be extended as far as the service requires.
elements.sent-2411-892: For example, if a service offers endpoints that require a new protocol but customer devices do not support that protocol, the provider might only use proxies at customer devices to upgrade connections for them.
elements.sent-2411-893: On the other hand, a service provider might consider an end-to-end secure overlay that starts from end-user devices and integrates with a service mesh to provide better request handling and resource management.
elements.sent-2411-894: This would require proxies at end-user devices, at the ingress point, and at service mesh components.
elements.sent-2411-895: All of these are valid use cases of the Hermes architecture, and to support them we consider various proxy deployment options as shown in Figure \ref{fig:proxy_configs .}
elements.sent-2411-896: The black box represents a physical machine.
elements.sent-2411-897: Proxies can be part of the application, e.g., service workers in web browsers \cite{service_workers , or can be deployed as a separate component outside of the application.}
elements.sent-2411-898: Proxies can also be shared between applications.
elements.sent-2411-899: Even if the device does not support proxies, they can be deployed inside the local network and devices can communicate with the proxy endpoint directly.
elements.sent-2411-900: We also decided to use user-space solutions on end-user devices as they provide wider compatibility and portability, as also seen in Wireguard userspace implementations \cite{boringtun .}
elements.sent-2411-901: Hermes DPs listen to UDP and TCP ports and process packets at UDP, TCP, and HTTP layers, ensuring that all traffic exiting the end-user device is either HTTP or encapsulated in HTTP.
elements.sent-2411-902: In this way, traffic along its path to the destination can be processed by SaPs solely based on its HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-903: Hermes SaPs can also process traffic based on UDP or TCP attributes if they are configured to, but the traffic remains HTTP.
elements.sent-2411-904: There are four main reasons why we chose HTTP as the carrier for every kind of traffic.
elements.sent-2411-905: First, we need to associate users with their traffic.
elements.sent-2411-906: This association either requires new protocols and designs, or it should be done using current protocols.
elements.sent-2411-907: HTTP headers have long been used for associating web traffic with user identities, and the Internet itself is engineered to provide best support for HTTP.
elements.sent-2411-908: Over 50\% of Internet traffic is HTTP(S) \cite{schumann_impact_2022, tsareva_decade_2023 and HTTP acts as the narrow waist of the Internet \cite{popa_http_2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-909: For instance, if we chose a custom UDP-based protocol to encapsulate data, it is possible that general UDP traffic would not get the same treatment as HTTP traffic in some access ISPs \cite{kuhlewind_manageability_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-910: Second, HTTP is highly extensible and adaptable to carry virtually any type of traffic.
elements.sent-2411-911: Indeed, HTTP can act as a unifying protocol for different traffic types: HTTP can be used to encapsulate UDP, IP, and Ethernet (RFC 9298 \cite{schinazi_proxying_2022 , RFC 9484 \cite{pauly_proxying_2023 , and \cite{sedeno_proxying_2024 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-912: These are all part of the MASQUE framework \cite{schinazi_masque_2024 , which extends HTTP's functionality and positions it as a versatile, general-purpose communication protocol well-suited for unified processing of heterogeneous traffic types.}
elements.sent-2411-913: In addition, HTTP/3 maps HTTP semantics onto QUIC, which natively supports multiplexed streams, and can also carry unreliable messages using the QUIC DATAGRAM extension \cite{rfc_9297, pauly_unreliable_2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-914: Third, by using HTTP headers, Hermes provides native support for custom namespaces and operates as a name-based communication layer rather than relying solely on location-based Internet mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-915: Namespaces can follow any structure, for example, a concatenation of strings and numbers or even an IP-like token.
elements.sent-2411-917: Alice's web browser is configured to forward HTTP requests to her dependent proxy.
elements.sent-2411-918: The DP forwards the request upstream without rewriting the host information (HTTP/1.1 Host header or HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 :authority).
elements.sent-2411-919: Overlay proxies along the path inspect the host information and route to the next destination accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-920: The request eventually reaches a proxy that can resolve DEV for Alice and forward it to the correct endpoint.
elements.sent-2411-922: First, overlay proxies can resolve DEV uniquely for each user, since identity is present in headers and can be used in routing decisions.
elements.sent-2411-923: Second, Alice's device does not rely on DNS to map DEV to an IP address.
elements.sent-2411-924: To compare with similar solutions, in Tailscale's MagicDNS, users can have custom namespaces that are converted to IP addresses using DNS resolution on the user's device \cite{magicdns .}
elements.sent-2411-925: Unlike MagicDNS, Hermes keeps names in application-layer headers and relies on overlay proxies to resolve names without performing DNS resolution on the end-user's device.
elements.sent-2411-926: Headers can also carry new semantics.
elements.sent-2411-927: For example, to broadcast a request to multiple overlay destinations, we can define a header such as X-Hermes-Broadcast: true.
elements.sent-2411-928: SaPs that see this header duplicate the request to all configured upstream destinations.
elements.sent-2411-929: This approach lets Hermes support a variety of use cases that require custom behavior without modifying applications.
elements.sent-2411-930: Fourth, using HTTP CONNECT \cite{fielding_hypertext_1997, thomson_http2_2022 and CONNECT-UDP \cite{schinazi_proxying_2022 , a client can tunnel an end-to-end secure connection through proxies without allowing those proxies to undetectably alter the protected payload.}}
elements.sent-2411-931: With CONNECT, the client sends the authority (host:port) to its configured proxy, the proxy opens a TCP connection to the target, returns 200 OK, and switches to blind byte forwarding.
elements.sent-2411-932: TLS over TCP inside this tunnel provides end-to-end confidentiality and integrity between client and destination.
elements.sent-2411-933: With CONNECT-UDP, the client is configured with a MASQUE URI template that carries target\_host and target\_port; the proxy extracts these values, opens a UDP socket to the target, and relays UDP payloads.
elements.sent-2411-934: End-to-end security then comes from the tunneled UDP protocol, for example QUIC or DTLS.
elements.sent-2411-935: These properties also enable shared third-party overlays, since a proxy cannot read or modify the inner TLS or QUIC payload without detection.
elements.sent-2411-936: Multiple tunnels can be chained, and proxies along the path can create or terminate tunnels as required; end-to-end protection remains intact.
elements.sent-2411-937: Finally, the overlay requires a control plane to manage the life cycle of proxy components and their configuration, monitoring, observations, and other aspects of management.
elements.sent-2411-938: This paper is mostly focused on the data-plane components and their specification, but we will review the control-plane components in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-939: Please also note that Hermes is not restricted to a specific version of HTTP, and choosing which version depends directly on service requirements.
elements.sent-2411-940: As long as proxies are reconfigurable, even the HTTP version in use can be updated to match changes in service and network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-941: Hermes main components are DPs and SaPs proxies, assisting components, and the control plane.
elements.sent-2411-942: This section looks into details of these components.
elements.sent-2411-943: The overlay proxies are software components and IP endpoints with IPv4 or IPv6 addresses and associated TCP and UDP ports.
elements.sent-2411-944: They process traffic using UDP, TCP, and HTTP header information.
elements.sent-2411-945: Hermes extends proxy capabilities with assisting components that enable IP traffic capture and processing, policy-based routing, advanced traffic management, and other tasks.
elements.sent-2411-946: Assisting components can be built into the proxy or deployed as separate processes.
elements.sent-2411-947: In fact, assisting components might perform complex functionality that would not be reasonable to merge with the proxy itself.
elements.sent-2411-948: If deployed separately, they must communicate directly with the proxy they assist.
elements.sent-2411-949: For example, a separate assisting component that creates a tunnel device should send captured IP packets to the local proxy, which then decides how to route them.
elements.sent-2411-950: Separate assisting components may or may not be co-located with the proxy on the same host, depending on their functionality.
elements.sent-2411-951: In all cases they must be configurable by the control plane and should communicate only through the proxy.
elements.sent-2411-952: As already mentioned, Hermes assumes two roles for proxies: dependent (DP) and standalone (SaP).
elements.sent-2411-953: As a core element of the architecture, end-user devices must connect to dependent proxies, but the inclusion of SaPs is optional.
elements.sent-2411-954: Please note that both types of proxies can create new tunnels, perform protocol translation, or have separate configuration for users based on their identities, and the architecture does not restrict them.
elements.sent-2411-955: Dependent proxies Dependent proxies accept traffic from end hosts, which includes the end-user device as the source or the destination of the traffic, and act as communication gateways to one or more applications or services, according to Figures \ref{fig:proxy1 to \ref{fig:proxy3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-956: The architecture mandates running at least one dependent proxy on the end-user side of communication to be able to manage the traffic from the source and also augment end-user traffic with overlay-related information.
elements.sent-2411-957: As discussed above, dependent proxies utilize HTTP headers to augment incoming traffic with overlay-specific information, which the overlay uses to determine subsequent actions.
elements.sent-2411-958: This information can include user identity, custom name-space information, or tunneling details, among other examples.
elements.sent-2411-959: Since Hermes proxies can rely on assisting components, when those components are external to the proxy the proxy must be able to understand their incoming traffic and even associate them with the appropriate headers.
elements.sent-2411-960: For example, if an assisting component enforces policies, the local proxy must be able to detect whether traffic is allowed or denied.
elements.sent-2411-961: As another example, to handle general IP packets, proxies need a tunnel device that can be created by the proxy itself (for example \cite{singbox ) or by a separate assisting component.}
elements.sent-2411-962: If the proxy can create a tunnel device, it can directly encapsulate traffic in HTTP, append the related headers, and forward it.
elements.sent-2411-963: If the proxy uses a separate assisting component, that component must encapsulate IP packets in TCP or UDP and deliver them to a specific port on the proxy.
elements.sent-2411-964: The port can be chosen based on the IP destination.
elements.sent-2411-965: For instance, traffic destined to 10.10.0.0/16 can be encapsulated in UDP and directed to UDP port 10000 on the proxy.
elements.sent-2411-966: The control plane needs to configure the assisting component to send such traffic to UDP port 10000, and configure the proxy to encapsulate data arriving on UDP port 10000 in HTTP while appending headers that indicate the destination network 10.10.0.0/16.
elements.sent-2411-967: Standalone proxies Standalone proxies act as software routers that route traffic between different sections of the overlay, similar to Figure \ref{fig:SaProxy .}
elements.sent-2411-968: These proxies can act as policy enforcers and enforce access control across Layers 3-7 between different overlay sections.
elements.sent-2411-969: It may accept traffic on one of its ports from other proxies, acting as a standalone proxy, while also communicating with a local application on another port, acting as a dependent proxy.
elements.sent-2411-970: The focus of this paper is the Hermes data plane.
elements.sent-2411-971: Here we briefly discuss the control-plane components.
elements.sent-2411-972: We also implemented various control-plane components, as we elaborate in Section \ref{imp , but the discussion about exact control plane functionalities and its interfaces is out of the scope of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-973: The control plane, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:controlPlane , consists of four key components: the overlay controller, infrastructure controller, configuration database, and observability and management tools.}
elements.sent-2411-974: The overlay controller The overlay controller is responsible for the placement, configuration, and lifecycle management of overlay proxies and their supporting components.
elements.sent-2411-975: It ensures that the overlay remains healthy and efficient by implementing various network management solutions as needed.
elements.sent-2411-976: The infrastructure controller The infrastructure controller serves as the interface between the overlay controller and the underlying infrastructure manager.
elements.sent-2411-977: When proxies are deployed on managed infrastructure, this component assists the control plane in deploying and managing proxies effectively.
elements.sent-2411-978: It communicates directly with the overlay controller to manage resources that are assigned to the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-979: Configuration database The configuration database stores the configurations of all overlay components that specify how they process traffic.
elements.sent-2411-980: The overlay controller writes to the configuration database to update the overlay configuration and, when necessary, pushes the latest configurations to the overlay components to ensure they stay up to date.
elements.sent-2411-981: The observability and monitoring component The observability and monitoring component collects and stores data on overlay status, resource usage, and end-user telemetry, among other metrics.
elements.sent-2411-982: Now that we have discussed the overlay components, we can define the users within the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-983: End-user An end-user device is the source of traffic passing through the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-984: Traffic may originate from IoT devices, mobile devices, or other systems.
elements.sent-2411-985: End-users have full control over their devices and can start or stop proxies and supporting components like any other user-space application.
elements.sent-2411-986: Service providers can only configure proxies through control-plane interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-987: Service providers These are organizations or individuals that use the overlay to enhance service delivery over the Internet and improve end-user experience.
elements.sent-2411-988: Overlay operator The overlay operator is the organization or individual who sets up the control plane and manages proxy configuration.
elements.sent-2411-989: The overlay operator is not necessarily the same as the service provider and may be a third party.
elements.sent-2411-990: In such cases, an overlay can be operated by a third party and used by multiple service providers to improve their service delivery.
elements.sent-2411-991: These third-party shared overlays are similar in business model to offerings such as Tailscale and Cloudflare Zero Trust.
elements.sent-2411-992: An overlay may also span multiple administrative domains, where independent operators collaborate to maintain a global-scale overlay while each controls the proxies within its own domain.
elements.sent-2411-993: In such cases, operators need only standardize a subset of the HTTP headers they exchange, which is far simpler than standardizing a new protocol.
elements.sent-2411-994: This is a practical advantage of using HTTP in the Hermes design.
elements.sent-2411-995: Although multi-operator overlays are feasible, this paper focuses on the single-operator model.
elements.subsection-2411-016: How Hermes features address the challenges?
elements.sent-2411-996: With the building blocks described above, Hermes addresses the fundamental challenges of service delivery over the Internet in the following ways.. End-to-end traffic management.
elements.sent-2411-997: Hermes provides fine-grained, end-to-end IP layer traffic control and observation through an overlay of proxies that extends to end-user devices.
elements.sent-2411-998: Deploying a proxy on the end-user device allows the organization to extend its control and processing capabilities directly to the user's device.
elements.sent-2411-999: This enables early and end-to-end control over user traffic from the moment it is generated, offering several significant advantages.
elements.sent-2411-1000: These include fine-grained and customer-specific traffic management, better load balancing and endpoint selection based on early insight into last-mile path characteristics, and the ability to perform local algorithms on user devices for improved traffic control.
elements.sent-2411-1001: Additionally, if the overlay is integrated with the service environment, such as a service mesh, end-user traffic can be traced from the moment it is generated, across the Internet, until it is consumed by the target services.
elements.sent-2411-1002: This simplifies distributed tracing and root cause identification for poor service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-1003: Since Hermes handles IP traffic and is not limited to TCP and HTTP traffic, this end-to-end control enables Hermes to replace legacy VPNs in remote access use cases, as we show in Section \ref{IP .. Backward compatibility.}
elements.sent-2411-1004: Proxies capture traffic and can process its header and payload if they are configured to.
elements.sent-2411-1005: Hermes provides a diverse range of techniques to provide backward compatibility.
elements.sent-2411-1006: Hermes proxies can provide automated service interface translation between different interface versions.
elements.sent-2411-1007: In addition, Hermes proxies can provide various protocol translation and tunneling techniques.
elements.sent-2411-1008: For example, a proxy can translate between HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/3, or it can tunnel UDP traffic over TCP and HTTP, although the tunneling techniques require proxies at each end to be configured in the same way.
elements.sent-2411-1009: In this way, the application logic remains simple; application traffic uses fixed proxy endpoints and does not need to update the protocols it uses or modify its communication logic.
elements.sent-2411-1010: Instead, the proxy component provides compatibility between application traffic and service definitions.
elements.sent-2411-1011: This simplifies application development, as whenever network and service requirements change, proxy components can be automatically updated to reflect changes without modifying the application logic.
elements.sent-2411-1012: In Section \ref{video , we demonstrate how an RTP-based video streaming service can achieve reliable, lossless streaming in a noisy network by tunneling UDP over HTTP, without requiring any modifications to the client or server implementations.}
elements.sent-2411-1013: Hermes provides an end-to-end overlay that can span all network segments and controls traffic at the IP layer and above.
elements.sent-2411-1014: Using assisting components that capture IP packets, tunnel them over HTTP, and carry IP metadata in HTTP headers, overlay proxies can enforce Layer 3 policies by evaluating the required information directly from headers.
elements.sent-2411-1015: Layer 3 policies specify which entities may communicate at the IP layer.
elements.sent-2411-1016: Proxies can also enforce Layer 4 policies, restricting which overlay entities may access specific port and protocol combinations, and Layer 7 policies, determining which application-level functions are accessible to an overlay entity.
elements.sent-2411-1017: Hermes therefore enables unified security policy enforcement across network layers and segments.
elements.sent-2411-1018: Please refer to our prior work \cite{farkiani_l3mesh for an example.}
elements.sent-2411-1019: Because traffic processing begins on the end-user device rather than only at the service edge, Hermes can enforce a unified privacy model.
elements.sent-2411-1020: By instantiating a virtual Layer 3 network from the end-user device to the service, Hermes can create a single, homogeneous Layer 3 policy domain across network segments; payloads can be encrypted on the device and decrypted only inside the target service process, enabling true end-to-end encryption for confidential and privacy-preserving computing.
elements.sent-2411-1021: The data remains routable because it is tunneled inside HTTP with headers that carry identity and destination metadata, without exposing payloads to overlay nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1022: In effect, traffic is treated under one unified security and privacy policy from its origin at the device, through ingress into the service provider domain, and onward to the target service.
elements.sent-2411-1023: Please refer to Section~\ref{IP for an example.. Adaptable communication layer.}
elements.sent-2411-1024: Proxies have end-to-end control over traffic, and the overlay controller can update their configuration to respond to changes in network and service conditions.
elements.sent-2411-1025: In this model, UDP, TCP, and HTTP proxying and tunneling are tools rather than constraints, since proxy behavior is policy-driven and reconfigurable.
elements.sent-2411-1026: For example, proxies and assisting components can be configured with custom algorithms for severe network disruption that outperform the default reliability features of standard transport protocols.
elements.sent-2411-1027: In Section \ref{intermittent we show that while direct TCP communication is infeasible in highly unstable networks \cite{farkiani_mitigating_2023 , a Hermes overlay with tuned proxy configuration achieved a 100\% delivery rate using TCP as the underlying transport.}}
elements.sent-2411-1028: Additionally, Hermes can serve as an efficient communication layer for experimental networking architectures by providing capabilities that are often missing from basic Internet delivery.
elements.sent-2411-1029: Many new architectures require features such as custom name resolution and reliable delivery to operate efficiently over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-1030: Hermes can offer these as common services, letting architectures leverage them without implementing their own native mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-1031: For example, an IP-based architecture can tunnel its traffic through Hermes, using HTTP headers for custom naming schemes and relying on Hermes for reliability.
elements.sent-2411-1032: In Section~\ref{NDN , we show that the Hermes can carry traffic between NDN nodes by mapping namespaces to HTTP headers while providing reliability and load balancing beneath NDN, reducing the need for NDN to rely on its native mechanisms for these functions.}
elements.sent-2411-1033: There are several additional aspects that must be considered before deploying the Hermes overlay.
elements.sent-2411-1034: A central question is: why would a service provider deliver its service via the Hermes overlay and assume the associated costs? Service providers can use Hermes to deliver better service quality to end-users.
elements.sent-2411-1035: Traffic can be delivered with the quality the provider specifies, rather than whatever last-mile conditions dictate.
elements.sent-2411-1036: End-to-end control over incoming traffic also enables global load balancing and end-to-end distributed tracing.
elements.sent-2411-1037: In addition, because the overlay can handle traffic at Layers 3-7 in an integrated way across network segments and network layers, it lets providers implement complex business policies efficiently in a unified and integrated way without stitching together multiple tools for each layer and segment.
elements.sent-2411-1038: Together, these capabilities improve service delivery and customer satisfaction while enhancing resource management, increasing profit, and reducing operational costs.
elements.sent-2411-1039: A potential solution, which we already discussed in the paper, is to use shared overlays that are offered by a third-party overlay provider.
elements.sent-2411-1040: In this way, one proxy instance is deployed on the end-user devices and the proxy is deployed and managed by the third-party.
elements.sent-2411-1041: Each service provider will then separately contract with the third-party overlay operator and will have interfaces to configure proxy according to their needs.
elements.sent-2411-1042: Therefore, instead of working with many proxy instances, end-user will only deal with one proxy.
elements.sent-2411-1043: This approach is similar to how Tailscale \cite{tailscale or Cloudflare ZeroTrust \cite{cloudflare_zerotrust is operating today.}}
elements.sent-2411-1044: As for the second question, it is important to highlight that Hermes acts as a service facilitator.
elements.sent-2411-1045: The answer to this question depends on the problem the service provider is facing, how many customers are affected, and what benefits Hermes can provide.
elements.sent-2411-1046: A service provider might even selectively route the traffic of certain services through Hermes while allowing other services to bypass it.
elements.sent-2411-1047: Does using Hermes improve or degrade performance? It depends.
elements.sent-2411-1048: Using an overlay of proxies inevitably adds per-connection processing overhead compared to operating without a proxy.
elements.sent-2411-1049: However, proxies can provide other benefits such as connection pooling and HTTP/2 or HTTP/3 stream multiplexing, which can reduce average end-to-end latency at scale, as we show in Section \ref{perf .}
elements.sent-2411-1050: Hermes requires HTTP-layer processing at dependent proxies to prepare user traffic for the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-1051: Hermes is designed to be portable and to run on end-user devices, where we assume the service provider controls only proxy configuration, not the device itself.
elements.sent-2411-1052: Therefore, design choices on the client side are limited.
elements.sent-2411-1053: Once traffic leaves the end-user device, it is under the service provider's control and can be processed as desired.
elements.sent-2411-1054: Because Hermes is HTTP-based, traffic must be processed at the HTTP layer at least once more at some proxy along the path, while other overlay proxies can use transport-layer processing to reduce per-hop overhead.

To address these questions, it's essential to consider the overlay operator, the entity responsible for managing the overlay.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Providing legacy application and protocol compatibility
elements.sent-2411-1119: In this section, we show how Hermes can address backward compatibility challenges without requiring modifications to existing client or server applications.
elements.sent-2411-1120: Because proxies act as IP endpoints, they can perform protocol translation (e.g., HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/3) or API translation (e.g., converting old request formats into new ones) transparently to clients.
elements.sent-2411-1121: Beyond protocol translation, Hermes can also enhance functionality without traffic translation.
elements.sent-2411-1122: For example, if end-users are limited to unreliable protocols, Hermes can provide reliability without translating the protocols into a different reliable protocol.
elements.sent-2411-1123: In this way, end-users benefit from reliability while continuing to use their unmodified applications.
elements.sent-2411-1124: The only change required is directing traffic to a local Hermes proxy endpoint instead of the original destination.
elements.sent-2411-1125: As a concrete example, consider video streaming over the RTP protocol \cite{schulzrinne_rtp_2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-1126: RTP over UDP is widely used on the Internet, for instance, by Zoom \cite{michel_enabling_2022 , Vimeo \cite{vimeo_rtp , and others \cite{rtp_ffmpeg, blair_how_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-1127: Although many streaming services use reliable protocols, RTP does not guarantee delivery or enforce packet ordering \cite{schulzrinne_rtp_2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-1128: Most implementations, therefore, add their own application-layer recovery mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-1129: For example, Zoom retransmits lost packets up to two times \cite{michel_enabling_2022 , whereas FFmpeg provides no recovery mechanisms out of the box.}
elements.sent-2411-1130: A customer using an RTP-based service over UDP reports degraded video quality.
elements.sent-2411-1131: Implementing a recovery layer would normally require modifying either the client or the server, an impractical solution if customers resist updates or if the streaming infrastructure is closed source.
elements.sent-2411-1132: Additionally, customers in different regions may have different reliability requirements.
elements.sent-2411-1133: For example, Zoom's two retries might suffice for one customer, but another may need more retries depending on network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-1134: We therefore ask: can Hermes enable reliable video delivery over RTP without any modifications to existing client or server implementations?.

elements.sent-2411-1178: This section examines how Hermes can act as an adaptable communication layer.
elements.sent-2411-1179: Reliable transport protocols react to communication failures according to their designs.
elements.sent-2411-1180: However, when multiple links fail along the path, and in extreme cases no end-to-end path exists at a given time, end-to-end transports fail simply because no continuous route is available to deliver traffic.
elements.sent-2411-1181: Such conditions arise in Delay Tolerant Networks (DTNs), and there is a substantial literature on protocols and approaches for these settings \cite{ginzboorg_message_2014, fall_dtn_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-1182: Adopting a DTN stack, however, typically requires changes to clients, servers, or network nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1183: Our goal is to provide DTN-like resilience without modifying applications.
elements.sent-2411-1184: Hermes enables TCP to deliver data by segmenting the path with proxies.
elements.sent-2411-1185: When proxies fail to transfer data to the upstream destination, they retry based on configured retry policies.
elements.sent-2411-1186: The service provider configures retry policies, including retry limits and backoff schedules, through the control plane based on service requirements and network conditions.
elements.sent-2411-1187: When the control plane is unreachable, proxies can fall back to local retry policies.
elements.sent-2411-1188: This ensures that retry behavior continues autonomously until coordination is restored.
elements.sent-2411-1189: The network between the user and the server is severely unstable, for example when nodes are mobile and there is no stable wireless coverage for all nodes.

elements.sent-2411-1192: Proxies are IP endpoints that can initiate and terminate TCP connections on each segment and control them according to local conditions.

elements.subsection-2411-020: Overcoming network degradation through adjusting overlay configuration
elements.sent-2411-1227: This section examines how Hermes can extend an end-to-end Layer 3 overlay across network segments.
elements.sent-2411-1228: The overlay can provide policy-based routing, and we show how quickly the overlay can be reconfigured to mitigate increases in latency.
elements.sent-2411-1229: These updates occur without disrupting underlying IP routing or requiring user actions, and they reduce end-to-end latency by updating network paths.
elements.sent-2411-1230: An organization allows employees to connect remotely to internal servers to perform daily tasks and access services.
elements.sent-2411-1231: The organization operates in two regions, one in Poland and one in the United States.
elements.sent-2411-1232: Each remote developer has access to three IP prefixes: 10.0.0.1/32 for a personal server, 10.0.0.2/32 for a shared service such as internal mail, and 128.252.0.0/16 for general internal servers.
elements.sent-2411-1233: Each user is assigned a dedicated virtual network slice, so two users can target the same IP address, for example 10.0.0.1, while the physical destination might differ and is resolved based on the user's identity.
elements.sent-2411-1234: The user to destination mapping is unique.
elements.sent-2411-1235: The organization needs a solution that enforces end-to-end policies and supports seamless updates to endpoints and address spaces.
elements.sent-2411-1236: In our scenario, a user who lives in Poland moves to the United States but continues to connect to an ingress and a personal server located in Poland.
elements.sent-2411-1237: The overlay controller and the user detect a latency increase.
elements.sent-2411-1238: The policy requires end-to-end latency to remain below a threshold.
elements.sent-2411-1239: If this threshold is violated, the control plane should adjust overlay configuration to improve performance.
elements.sent-2411-1240: In this case, the control plane chooses to relocate both the user's ingress and the user's personal server (10.0.0.1) to the United States.
elements.sent-2411-1241: A traditional setup can be built with WireGuard as the VPN client and server.
elements.sent-2411-1242: On the server, traffic from each user can be steered to a specific routing table to resolve destinations according to the user's assigned IP space, which provides per-user isolation.
elements.sent-2411-1243: Additional configuration, such as source NAT, may be required for a complete solution.
elements.sent-2411-1245: WireGuard and other standard VPN protocol traffic can be readily identified by middleboxes and may be blocked by ISPs along the path.
elements.sent-2411-1246: Updating the binding between a private IP and the physical host is operationally heavy.
elements.sent-2411-1247: It typically requires changes to routes, NAT, and firewall rules on the VPN gateway.

elements.sent-2411-1252: In this use case, we capture traffic at Layer 3 to create a virtual Layer 3 service over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-1253: The approach is to capture IP traffic using a tunnel device, encapsulate it in HTTP, attach HTTP headers that encode destination and user identity, and route traffic according to those headers.
elements.sent-2411-1254: This way, we treat the destination as an HTTP header and rely on the overlay's Layer 7 forwarding rules to determine packet destination.
elements.sent-2411-1255: Additionally, changing the binding between a private IP and a physical host only requires updating HTTP forwarding rules inside overlay proxies.
elements.sent-2411-1256: Therefore, IP traffic encapsulated in HTTP is routed across network segments by processing its HTTP headers until it reaches the destination.
elements.sent-2411-1257: After decapsulation, it is passed to the tunnel device at the destination to deliver it to the target process.
elements.sent-2411-1258: Because the Envoy proxies cannot create tunnel devices, we add an assisting component that interfaces with the proxy over UDP.
elements.sent-2411-1260: Tunnel devices on end hosts capture IP packets, encapsulate them in UDP, and deliver them to a proxy port.
elements.sent-2411-1261: In our prototype, this results in two nested tunnels: IP-in-UDP and UDP-in-HTTP.
elements.sent-2411-1262: The optimal design would use a single IP-in-HTTP tunnel from end-user to destination when proxy software supports tunnel interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-1264: Encapsulation of IP traffic in UDP.
elements.sent-2411-1265: Split tunneling that maps each destination network to a specific port on the user's dependent proxy that is configured to accept traffic to that destination network.
elements.sent-2411-1266: We implemented an assisting component that provides IP-in-UDP encapsulation and split tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-1267: Traffic arriving on each DP port is tagged with headers that identify the destination network.
elements.sent-2411-1268: The DP then encapsulates the UDP packets inside HTTP and appends user-related headers to bind destination addresses to the user identity.
elements.sent-2411-1269: It forwards the request to the next proxy, which consults HTTP headers to select the next hop.
elements.sent-2411-1270: This continues until the chain reaches a proxy that decapsulates UDP and hands it to an assisting component.
elements.sent-2411-1271: The assisting component then decapsulates IP and delivers the packets to the final destination.
elements.sent-2411-1272: If endpoint applications already use encryption, for example HTTPS or SSH, the overlay does not need to add link encryption between proxies.
elements.sent-2411-1273: Otherwise, the overlay can selectively enable TLS between specific proxy pairs.
elements.sent-2411-1274: The overlay also performs policy-based routing and authorization using HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-1275: When the control plane detects that user latency exceeds a configured threshold, it updates proxy configuration to steer traffic to a closer server.
elements.sent-2411-1276: The user continues to use the same IP address, and no user action is required.
elements.sent-2411-1277: The destination address remains fixed while routing decisions are handled by the overlay proxies.

elements.sent-2411-1319: We demonstrated that Hermes can provide IP routing by attaching the destination address and user identity as HTTP headers and processing them along the path.
elements.sent-2411-1320: Together with the prior use case, this shows that Hermes can control traffic at Layers 3-7.
elements.sent-2411-1321: A notable point in the IP use case is that the user continues to use the same destination IP address.
elements.sent-2411-1322: By capturing traffic at IP via a tunnel device and encapsulating it in HTTP, Hermes can route based on high-level policies rather than being limited to Layer-3 IP-based routing.
elements.sent-2411-1323: Although HTTP tunneling added up to 5ms of latency in our measurements, updates were smooth because we changed a Layer 7 routing rule rather than applying complex Layer 3 modifications.
elements.sent-2411-1324: From updating the configuration database to completing proxy hot restarts, each update completed in under 10 seconds, including the time for pushing configuration to proxies.
elements.sent-2411-1325: If proxy software could create tunnel devices and natively support IP-in-HTTP, such as CONNECT-IP from RFC 9484 \cite{pauly_proxying_2023 , configuration would be simpler and latency would likely be lower.}
elements.sent-2411-1326: However, we are not aware of any current implementation that provides both capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-1327: Hermes can serve as an adaptable communication layer by providing built-in support for global load balancing, name-based routing, reliable delivery, and policy-based routing.
elements.sent-2411-1328: Other architectures can leverage these features with minimal configuration to operate more effectively over the Internet.

elements.sent-2411-1330: A small organization has adopted the NDN architecture for several branches worldwide.
elements.sent-2411-1331: NDN nodes use Faces \cite{shi_what_2021 to connect over the Internet by encapsulating and decapsulating NDN packets over TCP or UDP so they can traverse IP networks.}
elements.sent-2411-1332: The organization chose UDP Faces, so NDN payload is encapsulated in UDP.
elements.sent-2411-1333: The organization exposes two namespaces, DEV and TEST, each served by multiple replicas.
elements.sent-2411-1334: It needs load balancing within each namespace and, specifically, it wants identity-aware load balancing, where requests are routed to replicas based on the requester's identity as well as the name prefix.
elements.sent-2411-1335: For example, requests for /TEST/res1 from user X should go to server1, while the same name from user Y should go to server2.
elements.sent-2411-1336: Prefix-based load balancing is achievable in NDN, but per-user steering is not available by default because forwarding is name-centric rather than user-centric.
elements.sent-2411-1337: In addition, exposing UDP ports on the public Internet without protection is risky, and an effective firewall would require NDN-aware filtering.
elements.sent-2411-1338: A practical solution is to use a Hermes overlay as a communication layer between NDN nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1339: The overlay encapsulates NDN over HTTP and maps the NDN namespace into HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-1340: It also adds other information, such as user identity, as headers.
elements.sent-2411-1341: Overlay proxies then route traffic and perform load balancing based on the namespace and user identity headers.
elements.sent-2411-1342: Because traffic is carried over HTTP, standard commercial firewalls and security tools can filter, rate limit, and protect the exposed endpoints using familiar approaches.
elements.sent-2411-1343: Since the organization is small, it uses a third-party Hermes overlay that allows it to configure dependent proxies.
elements.sent-2411-1344: The organization also configures its NDN clients to communicate with the dependent proxies and publishes its load balancing policy and endpoint addresses to the third-party operator.
elements.sent-2411-1345: Each NDN Forwarding Daemon (NFD) instance configures one UDP Face per namespace that targets a dedicated port on the local dependent proxy.
elements.sent-2411-1346: The DP encapsulates incoming NDN over UDP datagrams using CONNECT-UDP over HTTP/2 and attaches headers that carry the destination namespace and the user identity.
elements.sent-2411-1347: The overlay is operated by a third party, so the organization only needs to configure its NDN clients to send each namespace to the designated DP ports, and configure the DP, via the overlay control plane, to map these ports to the corresponding HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-1348: Standalone proxies in the third-party overlay read these headers and route traffic toward the organization's NDN servers.
elements.sent-2411-1350: The network configuration is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:ndn .}
elements.sent-2411-1351: The end-user used ndncatchunks \cite{ndncatput to request content from the DEV and TEST namespaces, and servers published content with ndnputchunks \cite{ndncatput .}}
elements.sent-2411-1352: Only the end-user and the servers ran NFDs.
elements.sent-2411-1353: The end-user's NFD was configured to send Interests for each namespace to a distinct port on its dependent proxy.
elements.sent-2411-1354: We observed that when using UDP Faces and targeting a UDP port of the proxy on the same host, NFD could not regulate its outgoing rate.
elements.sent-2411-1355: To address this, we deployed the DP on a separate machine, allowing NFD to regulate its outgoing rate.
elements.sent-2411-1356: Running the DP off-host is one of the deployment modes Hermes supports and allows the architecture to fit a wide range of use cases and operational constraints.
elements.sent-2411-1357: When the local proxy received the UDP packets, it tunneled them over HTTP and appended two headers: service: NDN and a namespace header, either namespace: DEV or namespace: TEST, depending on the ingress port.
elements.sent-2411-1358: In this way, NDN namespaces were mapped to HTTP headers.
elements.sent-2411-1359: The local proxy also added user identity as an HTTP header.
elements.sent-2411-1360: The proxy then forwarded the data to SaP1 closer to the user.
elements.sent-2411-1361: This SaP was configured to partially process headers, so it only processed the service: NDN header and ignored the rest.
elements.sent-2411-1362: It then forwarded the traffic related to the NDN architecture to SaP2, which is responsible for parsing the namespace and user headers.
elements.sent-2411-1363: SaP2 distributed the traffic among the HTTP proxies colocated with the NFDs at the destination based on namespace and user identity.
elements.sent-2411-1364: The DP proxies then terminated the HTTP tunnel, decapsulated the UDP traffic, and delivered it to the local NFDs.
elements.sent-2411-1365: In the direct NDN connection, shown in the bottom of Figure \ref{fig:ndn , the client knew the addresses of the servers, and its NFD was configured with those addresses.}
elements.sent-2411-1366: The ndncatchunks tool was configured to always request fresh content and used a fixed interest pipeline size of 20.
elements.sent-2411-1367: For the 0KB file, the pipeline size was set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-1368: Although the Hermes approach is up to 12\% slower than the direct approach when downloading large file sizes and its latency is slightly higher, it provides various load-balancing features.
elements.sent-2411-1369: This facilitates the development and usage of the NDN architecture even when NDN uses UDP faces.
elements.sent-2411-1370: This approach greatly simplifies deployment and management of NDN and other networking architectures over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-1371: In the previous section, we used open-source tools to show how the Hermes data plane can be easily built.
elements.sent-2411-1372: We also used Envoy because it is a feature-rich proxy, though it may incur higher overhead than lighter alternatives such as Linkerd \cite{linkerd_bench .}
elements.sent-2411-1373: Generally, the performance of a Hermes overlay depends on the number of proxies on the end-to-end path, the processing performed at each proxy, and the proxy implementation, as discussed in Sections~\ref{perfReview and~\ref{imp .}}
elements.sent-2411-1374: Note that, as discussed earlier, Hermes requires HTTP processing on the end-user device because heterogeneous traffic types must be tunneled through HTTP for overlay routing.
elements.sent-2411-1375: Downstream overlay proxies do not all need to perform HTTP processing.
elements.sent-2411-1376: They can make routing decisions at lower layers until traffic reaches a proxy that requires HTTP inspection.
elements.sent-2411-1377: As a result, an overlay chain of proxies can begin with HTTP proxying at the end-user device, continue with Layer 4 processing across intermediate hops, and then invoke HTTP processing again at a later proxy when needed.
elements.sent-2411-1378: Additionally, the service provider needs to decide on the extent of the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-1379: Hermes uses user-space proxies on end-user devices to preserve portability across platforms.
elements.sent-2411-1380: However, when traffic reaches the managed infrastructure, the service provider can end the overlay and use kernel-based Layer 4 processing with eBPF to improve performance instead of user-space proxies (similar to Cilium \cite{cilium-mesh ).}
elements.sent-2411-1381: Overlays of Layer 7 proxies are frequently used in service environments because they provide various benefits.
elements.sent-2411-1382: For example, Meta's ServiceRouter architecture uses millions of Layer 7 proxies to route billions of requests per second across thousands of services \cite{saokar_servicerouter_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-1383: Since our prototype builds an overlay of Envoy proxies, and because Envoy is widely used in service mesh implementations such as Istio \cite{istioEnvoy and Consul \cite{consul , studies that investigated the performance of these implementations are relevant to Hermes performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-1384: Zhu et al. \cite{zhu_dissecting_2023 evaluated Envoy proxying overhead in service meshes that deploy proxies in sidecar mode, where each pod has its own proxy and both source and destination traffic are redirected through it.}
elements.sent-2411-1385: They reported up to 30\% overhead for TCP proxying and up to 129\% for HTTP parsing.
elements.sent-2411-1386: Saxena et al. \cite{saxena_copper_2025 observed similar effects, noting that end-to-end latency increased from 9.2ms to 27ms across a four-service chain.}
elements.sent-2411-1387: Because of the noticeable overhead in sidecar-based service meshes, newer designs such as Ambient Mode \cite{sidecar_ambient, maturing-ambient , Canal Mesh \cite{canal , and Cilium Mesh \cite{cilium-mesh have been proposed.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1388: Our design differs from conventional service meshes in three key respects.
elements.sent-2411-1389: It begins on end-user devices and may optionally merge with a downstream service mesh, depending on the application.
elements.sent-2411-1390: Because we deploy across heterogeneous end-user devices, we cannot rely on redirection techniques typical of service meshes on the end-user device.
elements.sent-2411-1391: We handle the full TCP/IP stack via tunneling mechanisms and assisting components, whereas most service meshes focus primarily on TCP and HTTP.
elements.sent-2411-1392: In service mesh environments, the traffic redirection strategy and whether security features such as HBONE \cite{hbone are enabled also affect measurements.}
elements.sent-2411-1393: Despite these differences, the reported overheads above are relevant to our architecture and align with the end-to-end results reported in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-1394: Additionally, although we used a small number of users to keep the focus on the application of Hermes, the system follows similar scaling practices to service meshes: the number of standalone proxies and dependent proxies at the destination can be increased horizontally to absorb higher traffic volumes, and better proxy placement strategies can improve system performance (e.g., \cite{saxena_copper_2025 ).}
elements.sent-2411-1395: This section examines an important aspect of the architecture that, to the best of our knowledge, has not been studied in depth: the overhead of various HTTP proxying and tunneling techniques.
elements.sent-2411-1396: These mechanisms are core to Hermes because they enable heterogeneous traffic to be carried over the overlay.
elements.sent-2411-1397: To isolate the impact of tunneling and proxying, we configure proxies to perform only the minimal functions required for those mechanisms and exclude application-specific processing.

elements.sent-2411-1405: CONNECT proxying: The client sends an HTTP/1.1 CONNECT request to its dependent proxy, which forwards it to the server proxy.

elements.sent-2411-1414: Simple HTTP proxying: The client issues HTTP/1.1 GET requests to its dependent proxy, which forwards them to server proxy without extra processing.
elements.sent-2411-1415: Baseline is direct HTTP request to server.
elements.sent-2411-1417: Although completion times increase, ranging from 4\% for CONNECT proxying to 63\% for TCP tunneling, the absolute delays remain small: measured proxy overhead spans from 0.67 ms for HTTP proxying to 1.38 ms for UDP tunneling.
elements.sent-2411-1418: These findings indicate that the tunneling and proxying techniques at the core of our architecture add only modest latency.
elements.sent-2411-1419: Note that we measured tunneling and proxying with minimal proxy configuration.
elements.sent-2411-1420: More complex HTTP processing tailored to specific use cases would increase overhead, consistent with prior studies we reviewed.

Standard deviation is shown as error bars.} \label{fig:performance_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-1421: In addition to single-request analysis, we evaluated parallel requests scaled from 200 to 1000.
elements.sent-2411-1422: Each request represents a unique client, and thus we emulated a scenario in which requests from up to 1000 clients arrived at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-1424: In all scenarios, end-to-end latency increases as concurrency rises, and no request failures were observed.
elements.sent-2411-1425: With 1000 requests, the baseline approaches are 18.4\% and 110.5\% slower than the proxy approach for HTTP proxying and TCP tunneling, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1426: In contrast, for CONNECT and UDP, the baseline is 23.2\% and 72.2\% faster than the proxy approach.
elements.sent-2411-1427: Baseline UDP has neither reliability nor connection setup, while tunneling through HTTP introduces both reliability semantics and connection establishment on the proxy path.
elements.sent-2411-1428: Even if we used HTTP/3 with QUIC datagrams \cite{rfc_9297, pauly_unreliable_2022 , this would remove reliability semantics, but the QUIC connection still requires setup and adds TLS 1.3 cryptographic overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-1429: In the proxied CONNECT setup, the client sends an HTTP/1.1 CONNECT to the client proxy, which forwards it to the server proxy.
elements.sent-2411-1430: The server proxy opens a TCP connection to the backend and returns HTTP 200.
elements.sent-2411-1431: After that, the client performs the TLS handshake directly with the backend through the tunnel.
elements.sent-2411-1432: This process has high per-flow overhead, whereas the baseline simply establishes a direct client-to-server TLS connection.
elements.sent-2411-1433: Despite this overhead, TCP tunneling over HTTP outperforms direct TCP because the tunnel uses HTTP/2: each new tunneled request becomes a new HTTP/2 stream between the proxies instead of establishing a fresh end-to-end TCP connection for every request.
elements.sent-2411-1434: HTTP proxying also outperforms direct HTTP due to Envoy connection pooling, which keeps upstream connections open and reuses them, thereby reducing per-request handshake cost.
elements.sent-2411-1435: Note that Table~\ref{tab:perf reports single-connection overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-1436: HTTP/2 connection establishment adds fixed setup cost that raises single-request latency, but tunneling over HTTP/2 benefits workloads with many requests.
elements.sent-2411-1437: Therefore, although proxies increase single-connection overhead, they can reduce average end-to-end latency at scale by amortizing connection setup and leveraging HTTP/2 stream multiplexing and connection pooling.
elements.sent-2411-1438: All of these advantages or disadvantages depend on proxy implementation, the processing they perform, and the number of proxies in the path.
elements.sent-2411-1439: This paper presented Hermes, a general-purpose, portable, and adaptable networking architecture that addresses fundamental challenges in efficient service delivery over the Internet.
elements.sent-2411-1440: Hermes extends an overlay of reconfigurable proxies from end-user devices to service endpoints and decouples networking logic from application logic, enabling adaptation to changing service and network conditions without modifying applications.
elements.sent-2411-1441: The architecture provides unified solutions to four critical challenges: (i) end-to-end traffic management by extending service-provider control to end-user devices; (ii) backward compatibility through protocol translation and tunneling; (iii) consistent security and privacy via unified control across network layers and segments; and (iv) an adaptable communication layer that complements standard protocols to meet service requirements.
elements.sent-2411-1442: Through our evaluation, we showed that Hermes enables backward compatibility by tunneling traffic over HTTP, achieves a 100\% delivery success rate in intermittent connectivity scenarios where direct TCP fails by moving retry control into proxies, supports Layer 3 policy-based traffic routing, and serves as an efficient substrate for experimental architectures such as NDN.
elements.sent-2411-1443: Performance measurements indicate that while Hermes improves various performance measures, it can add end-to-end latency.
elements.sent-2411-1444: Across multiple use cases, we demonstrated that the benefits Hermes delivers outweigh this processing overhead.
elements.sent-2411-1445: Additionally, the isolated HTTP tunneling and proxying overhead, as a core part of the overlay, is modest, typically under 2ms per hop.
elements.sent-2411-1446: Finally, we showed although proxies increase one-hop single-connection overhead, they can amortize this cost at scale through connection pooling and HTTP/2 multiplexing, thereby lowering end-to-end latency relative to direct no-proxy baselines.
elements.sent-2411-1447: Future work will develop control-plane algorithms for dynamic overlay optimization and investigate AI-driven management to automate operations and improve reliability.
elements.sent-2411-1448: We also plan to extend the control-plane implementation and design configuration languages that support integrated Layers 3-7 policy definition across network segments.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran .}
